summary: This codebase is genuinely impressive — polished visual storytelling, a rich feature set, and clear care put into the details. The custom SVG character avatars, vibe-driven behavior, and interactive office scene are delightful. That said, 'delightful' doesn't mean 'done': there are real accessibility gaps, a performance anti-pattern baked into every single data fetch, and a sneaky TASK_TAG_DEFS inconsistency that means the office sprint board and the dashboard worker cards aren't actually speaking the same language. Plus someone put FREQ_STOPWORDS inside the component body and I will be losing sleep over it.

suggestions.title: TASK_TAG_DEFS inconsistency: sprint board misses hotfixes, broken pipelines, and more
suggestions.description: App.jsx defines its own TASK_TAG_DEFS with rich regex patterns (e.g. /fix|bug|hotfix|broken|error/i for 'Bug'). Office.jsx imports the simplified constants.js version (just { key: 'Bug', emoji: '🔴' }) and builds regex by keyword name alone: /\bBug\b/i. A task like 'hotfix authentication endpoint' or 'pipeline broken after merge' will show a 🔴 Bug tag on the worker card in Dashboard, but register ZERO on the Sprint Board in Office. The two should use the same source of truth. Fix: move the full TASK_TAG_DEFS with patterns into constants.js, import in both App.jsx and Office.jsx, and remove the local redefinition.

suggestions.title: Cache-busting ?t=Date.now() on every poll defeats HTTP caching entirely
suggestions.description: fetchWorkersFromGitHub, fetchActivityFromGitHub, and fetchIdeasFromGitHub all append ?t=Date.now() to their URLs. This ensures every 30-second poll is a fully fresh network request — no browser cache, no CDN cache, no conditional GET. On GitHub Pages with raw.githubusercontent.com, the response will never serve from cache. With 5 workers watching, that's a minimum of 6 cache-missed requests per minute per user. Remove the timestamp query param and rely on the Last-Modified / ETag headers that GitHub's CDN already provides, or switch to Cache-Control: no-cache with conditional fetch (If-None-Match). Bandwidth and latency both win.

suggestions.title: data-tooltip on character buttons is invisible to screen readers
suggestions.description: Worker characters (the clickable .char elements) use data-tooltip='Name · task · click to inspect' for their CSS-driven tooltip. That attribute is not read by screen readers. The element has role='button' and tabIndex=0, so keyboard users will focus it expecting to understand what it does — but there's no aria-label or aria-describedby pointing at the tooltip content. Fix: replace data-tooltip with aria-label (or aria-describedby + a visually-hidden span) so the full context — name, task, deep-work status, 'click to inspect' — is announced on focus.

suggestions.title: FREQ_STOPWORDS Set is recreated on every render of Office component
suggestions.description: Office.jsx line ~1369 declares 'const FREQ_STOPWORDS = new Set([...])' inside the Office function body. This allocates a new Set object on every render, even though the contents never change. The wordFreqPills useMemo correctly depends on it, but since FREQ_STOPWORDS is a new reference on every render, the memo's closure reference is stable (the Set is in the memo callback scope) — but the allocation itself is wasted work. Simple fix: hoist FREQ_STOPWORDS to module scope, alongside the other module-level constants like VACANT_ADS and PING_REACTIONS.

suggestions.title: ConferenceTable has two mutually exclusive useEffect intervals that should be one
suggestions.description: Office.jsx lines ~1028-1034 have two separate useEffect hooks that both set up a 30-second setTick interval for the conference table. The first returns early if meetingWorkers.length < 2 (runs only during meetings), the second returns early if meetingWorkers.length >= 2 (runs only outside meetings). They're mutually exclusive, so only one fires at a time — but this is needlessly confusing and doubles the effect bookkeeping. Merge into a single useEffect with a single setInterval that runs regardless of meeting state, or clarify via a comment why they're split if there's an intentional reason.

suggestions.title: completionToast and plantToast aria-live regions missing aria-atomic='true'
suggestions.description: Both the worker completion toast (App.jsx) and the plant milestone toast (Office.jsx) use aria-live='polite' without aria-atomic='true'. Without aria-atomic, screen readers may announce partial DOM changes mid-update — e.g. just the worker name before the full message is rendered. Adding aria-atomic='true' ensures the entire toast content is announced as a single atomic unit. While you're in there: the completionToast div is positioned via inline style — consider a shared .toast-overlay class for consistency and easier theme maintenance.

suggestions.title: isTouchDevice comment claims 'stays fresh on hybrid devices' but useMemo with [] never re-evaluates
suggestions.description: App.jsx line 818 has the comment '// Evaluated inside the component (via useMemo) to stay fresh on hybrid devices' but the implementation is useMemo(() => window.matchMedia(...).matches, []) with an empty dependency array. This runs exactly once on mount and never updates. On a hybrid touchscreen/trackpad device, if the user switches input modes, isTouchDevice stays stale. Either: (a) remove the misleading comment and accept the once-on-mount behavior (probably fine for most use cases), or (b) actually listen to the pointer media query change event via a proper useState + useEffect pattern.

suggestions.title: Keyboard users can't pin/hold the character hover card
suggestions.description: On touch devices, clicking a character opens a 'pinned card' with a dismiss button — persistent and readable. On desktop, the hover card shows on mouseenter/focus and hides on mouseleave/blur. But if a keyboard user Tabs to the next element, blur fires and the card disappears before they can finish reading it. Touch users got pinned cards as a gesture fallback, but keyboard users got nothing — they're worse off. Fix: when focus enters a .char button, also set pinnedCard=true (not just hovered=true), and only clear it on Escape or when focus leaves the entire character+card area. The dismiss button already exists in pinnedCard mode.

suggestions.title: WorkerCard memo gets no benefit when activityEntries array reference changes every render
suggestions.description: WorkerCard is wrapped in React.memo with no custom comparison function. In App.jsx, activityEntries are passed as activityByWorker.get(w.name) ?? []. The activityByWorker Map is recomputed in a useMemo that depends on [activityLog, activeWorkers, fadingMap]. Every time activityLog updates (every 30s poll), a new Map is created, and .get() returns a new array reference — which memo's default shallow equality will treat as a changed prop, re-rendering every card. Fix: either add a custom comparison function to WorkerCard that deep-compares activityEntries by timestamp, or memoize the per-worker entry arrays outside the render.

suggestions.title: after-hours vibe doesn't account for time-of-day when workers ARE present
suggestions.description: getTeamVibe() only returns 'after-hours' when workers.length === 0. If someone (cough, Dave) is grinding at 2am and workers.length > 0, the vibe will be 'in-flow' or 'crushing' — not 'after-hours'. The header greeting already checks time (currentHour < 5 || currentHour >= 22 → '🌃 Burning the midnight oil'), so the concept exists. Suggestion: add a time check in getTeamVibe() — if it's outside business hours AND vibe would otherwise be 'in-flow', downgrade to 'after-hours'. Dave's CISO title and the midnight sky deserve to appear together.

suggestions.title: VIBE_RANK and VIBE_LABELS_MAP are duplicated across App.jsx, Office.jsx is not affected — but maintenance risk exists
suggestions.description: VIBE_RANK is defined in App.jsx (line ~586) and used for sparkline sorting and confetti logic. computeDailySummary also has a local VIBE_LABELS_MAP. If a new vibe key is added, there are multiple places to update: VIBE_RANK, VIBE_COLORS, VIBE_VERSIONS, VIBE_WHITEBOARD, CLOCK_COMMENTS, DOOR_ICONS, etc. These are scattered across App.jsx, Office.jsx, and constants.js. Consider moving VIBE_RANK and the vibe key enum to constants.js as the single source of truth, to prevent future drift.

suggestions.title: Sprint board tag matching is too strict on word boundaries — 'Refactoring' won't match 'Refactor'
suggestions.description: Office.jsx builds sprint board tag regexes as /\bRefactor\b/i. A task that says 'Refactoring the auth module' won't match because \b requires the word to end there. App.jsx's pattern /refactor|optimiz|benchmark|perf.../i has no boundary, so it matches correctly. After fixing the TASK_TAG_DEFS inconsistency (suggestion #1), this resolves itself. Flagging separately so it's not lost in the shuffle.

suggestions.title: Activity log shows max 20 items but filter counts show total unfiltered count — misleading
suggestions.description: The activity log header shows e.g. 'Latest 20 of 47 events' when unfiltered — accurate. But when filtered (e.g. 'Errors'), it shows '3 of 3 events (filtered)' — also accurate. The subtle issue: the displayed entries are entries.slice(0,20).reverse() applied BEFORE filtering in ActivityLog component, but filteredActivityEntries is computed by filtering activityLog (up to 50 items) first, THEN slice(0,20) happens inside ActivityLog. So the count in the header ('3 of 3') refers to pre-sliced filtered entries, and if there are > 20 filtered entries, the header could say '20 of 20 events (filtered)' when there are really more. Audit the slice vs filter ordering to ensure the count label is always correct.

suggestions.title: Ghost workers (OOO) have no 'last active task' context — missed storytelling opportunity
suggestions.description: Ghost characters show an OOO bubble on hover with their role and 'Last seen: Xh ago'. But the last activity entry for that worker (which exists in activityEntries) could show their last task — 'Last seen: 3h ago, was fixing the auth bug'. The ghost bubble already does the work of finding the lastEvent timestamp; adding lastEvent.message to the display is a one-liner that adds real personality. Carlos's ghost shouldn't just say 'Backend Engineer' — he should say 'Backend Engineer · last seen: In the logs'.
